data.title: Allspring Core Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG) returned 2.03% during the third quarter of 2025. Yields across the curve were lower throughout the period after another quarter of tariff news and labor concerns, which drove the Federal Reserve (Fed) to finally cut rates.
  • 10-year U.S. Treasury yields fell by 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) to end the quarter at 4.15%, down from 4.57% where it ended 2024. The 2s/10s curve steepened 3 bps throughout the quarter, ending at 54 bps. The option-adjusted spread on the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Index (LBUSTRUU) tightened by 9 bps during the third quarter to 70 bps over U.S. Treasuries.

Market review

Growth in the U.S. economy has stabilized, with uncertainty remaining high. Following a slowdown in the first half of the year, personal consumption and private investment were at least stable exiting the third quarter. Personal incomes have demonstrated resiliency, supporting consumption, and public spending and private investment have contributed modestly to growth. Inventories and net exports have grown modestly after substantial shocks early in the year, and auto sales stayed strong ahead of the expiration of tax credits.

Measures of inflation have improved slightly, and the outlook for tariffs still seems uncertain, even as inflation expectations remain anchored. Core personal consumption expenditures inflation has only modestly improved on a monthly basis, resulting in a still-high 2.9% year-over-year rate. Services and housing inflation has been slow to improve, and goods have contributed to inflation, with some categories now showing impacts from tariffs.

Financial markets gained additional strength from the prospect of Fed cuts and enthusiasm around investments in artificial intelligence. Incoming economic data has looked more stable after an orderly growth slowdown but prospective risks seem to be rising again. Longer-term, market participants will need to contemplate the prospects of an ongoing U.S. economic slowdown, inflation risks, increasing data dependency, federal budget pressures in future years, a potentially weaker dollar, and a shift away from growth led by consumption to a more balanced picture of savings versus investment. Bond yields, particularly in the front and intermediate curve points, continue to look attractive, yet term premiums and risk spreads should remain volatile.

Portfolio Positioning - Portfolio Attribution* And Strategy

Security selection—credit

In credit, both the sector overweight and security selection contributed to performance during the quarter. Key contributors were concentrated in the foreign agency and sovereigns subsectors. In foreign agency, overweights to Saudi Aramco and Bank Gospodarstwa Krajowego were the primary drivers of performance, among others. In sovereigns, an overweight to Paraguay and Mexico contributed to performance. Additional single-name contributors included an overweight to Pacific Gas & Electric (utilities), Broadcom (technology), and NTT (communications). Key detractors were concentrated in the health care, pipelines, and utilities subsectors. In health care, an overweight to Eli Lilly (LLY) and UnitedHealth (UNH) detracted from performance. In pipelines, an overweight to Energy Transfer (ET) detracted. In utilities, an overweight to Exelon (EXC) also detracted from performance. Additional single-name detractors included an underweight to Panama (sovereigns) and an overweight to Charter Communications (communications), Citigroup (U.S. banks), and Meta (communications).

Security selection—agency mortgages

In agency mortgages, the sector overweight as well as positioning and security selection in pass-throughs have been the primary drivers of performance. Mortgage spreads tightened in materially this quarter, supported by a continued decline in volatility and robust investor demand, which benefited our overweight position. In security selection, the overweight to seasoned 30-year 3.0s has been the primary driver of performance on strong coupon and story performance. The underweight to Ginnie Mae mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and 15-year MBS also contributed modestly, as both subsectors lagged the broader tightening. As for detractors, positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations and select pass-through coupons detracted. Spreads on both fixed- and floating-rate structures lagged the broader sector tightening this quarter. The underweight to discount 30-years and overweight to production coupon 30-years detracted moderately as well.

Security selection—securitized

During the third quarter, our sector overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) contributed to performance as spreads tightened. Security selection across several ABS subsectors, including credit card ABS, auto loan ABS, device payment plan ABS, and Federal Family Education Loan Program student loans, also contributed while holdings of certain private credit student loan ABS and non-qualified mortgage residential MBS were small detractors on a risk-adjusted basis. Within commercial mortgage-backed securities, sector positioning detracted while holdings in single-asset/single-borrower and 5- and 10-year conduit positions contributed.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used statistics for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index is a broad base, market capitalization-weighted bond market index representing intermediate term investment grade bonds traded in the United States. You cannot invest directly in an index. The Fund uses the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Index for performance comparison. The investments of the Sub-Fund may deviate significantly from the components of and their respective weightings in the benchmark.

Definition of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMI-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund's duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond's (or fund's) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled intermediate core bond funds, the Core Bond Fund received 3 stars among 430 funds, 3 stars among 382 funds, and 3 stars among 288 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. data.title: Selling Starbucks And More Trades--November Dividend Income Report
data.description:
In 2016, I made a life-changing decision: I took a sabbatical, put my family in a small RV, and we drove all the way to Costa Rica.

Upon my return in 2017, I officially quit my job as a private banker at National Bank and started working full-time on my baby: Dividend Stocks Rock. I also decided to manage my pension account held at the National Bank. I’ve built and managed this portfolio publicly since 2017 to create and track a real-life case study.

In August 2017, I received $108,760.02 in a locked retirement account. Locked means I can’t add capital to the account, and growth is only generated through capital gains and dividends. I don’t report this portfolio’s results to brag about my returns or to suggest you follow my lead. My purpose was solely to share with our members how I manage my portfolio with all the good and the bad that inevitably takes place each month. I hope you have learned and will continue to learn from my experiences managing this portfolio.

Lots of trades

2025 was an exciting year for me regarding my investments. This is the year I started having more capital to allocate to my portfolios. Over the past few years, I’ve reinvested most of my money into my best investment ever: Dividend Stocks Rock. While it paid off tremendously, it’s time to think about increasing my liquid assets (since I have no intention of selling the business!).

By adding more capital (increasing my Smith Manoeuvre from $500 to $750/month and starting a new account that is not reported here), I also added a few more names to my holdings. I hit my limit of 40 not too long ago, and that rang a bell: it’s time to do a full review.

I explained my process last month in this very newsletter, so I’ll spare you the details and focus on the results.

Performance in Review

Let’s start with the numbers as of December 9th, 2025 (before the bell):

Original amount invested in September 2017 (no additional capital added): $108,760.02.

  • Current portfolio value: $323,127.52
  • Dividends paid: $5,227.85 (TTM)
  • Average yield: 1.62%
  • 2024 performance: +26.00%
  • VFV.TO = +35.24%, XIU.TO = +20.72%
  • Dividend growth: +12.22%

Total return since inception (September 2017 - November 2025): +197.10%

Annualized return (97 months): 14.42%

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV.TO)(VFV:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 16.55% (total return 244.90%)

iShares S&P/TSX 60 ETF (XIU.TO)(XIU:CA)(IUTSF) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 12.73% (total return 163.50%)

Small is beautiful

After my clean-up, I’m now down to 34 positions across all my portfolios. I always want to keep between 30 and 40 stocks for two simple reasons:

  1. I want each position to be meaningful and have an impact on my total returns.
  2. I always want to have my two hands on the wheel and be in full control. I can’t do that if I have 50+ stocks.

Again, this is my rule, and it’s not the only way to manage a portfolio. My goal with this report is to share my views and my strategies. I suggest you take what you like and discard what doesn’t apply to your situation. There are thousands of ways to manage your investments successfully. Build your own rules, and you’ll be happier with your results.

Smith Manoeuvre clean-up

This is where I have the most trades! I first started the Smith Manoeuvre with the intention of generating a decent yield (around 4%). But I quickly realized that I was more comfortable focusing on my main strategy of picking strong dividend growers.

Sell Canadian Tire (CTC.A.TO)(CTA.A:CA)(CDNTF)(CDNAF)

Sell Nutrien (NTR.TO)(NTR:CA)(NTR)

I would have never considered having Canadian Tire and Nutrien in my pension plan account.

While they are both great companies and I made a profit on both trades, they are just not a 100% fit with my core strategy. For that reason, I decided to get rid of both and align my holdings with the rest of my investments.

Buy Waste Connections (WCN.TO)(WCN:CA)(WCN)

With the extra capital, I decided to bolster one of my existing positions: Waste Connections. This is an excellent defensive stock, and I have the chance to buy it on the current price weakness. You will notice that the WCN price sometimes goes nowhere for a while. That’s why I’m not worried about the underperformance in 2025.

Buy Intact Financial (IFC.TO)(IFC:CA)(IFCZF)

The rest of my capital went to a new position that was highlighted in my buy list not too long ago: Intact Financial. IFC is a leader in property and casualty insurance in Canada with expansion opportunities in the UK and the US.

Increasing to $1,000/month starting in January

I’m doubling down on my Smith Manoeuvre strategy and will increase the monthly capital from $750 to $1,000. This was one of my goals in 2025, but I had to act diligently and make sure I could afford it. When you play with leverage, you play with fire.

Leverage amplifies everything: your good returns and your worst nightmares.

While I have a very high risk tolerance, I’m not a gambler. I’ve reviewed my financial plan for the next 6 years to ensure it fits within my global strategy.

Why six years? That’s an odd number, is it not?

In 6 years, I’ll be 50, and two of my three children will not live with us anymore (#3 will be 20). This will be the end of a season in my life and the beginning of a new one. I still don’t know for sure what this season will look like, but there will be several opportunities. Right now, I’m in a season of “financial consolidation” where many of my past moves are paying off, and it’s time to secure my financial future and invest as much as possible.

Pension portfolio optimization

As you know, I don’t like to make many trades. I prefer to buy and hold for a long time. But that doesn’t mean I’m blind to my portfolio performance. Hence, I have made three trades of late.

Sell Starbucks (SBUX)

This one should not come as a surprise. Starbucks had 3 years to change my mind. The dividend triangle has weakened since 2022, especially EPS. Management has a plan, but the team is more optimistic than the numbers indicate. Part of my investment thesis about SBUX was its impressive growth in China. This narrative isn’t true anymore (SBUX even sold 60% of its stake in the Chinese business). Finally, the latest dividend increase announcement (+1.64% vs. a high-single-digit growth rate in the past 5 years) was the final nail in the coffin.

Buy Automatic Data Processing (ADP)

Part of the proceeds from Starbucks went to my position in ADP. This company runs a tight ship with a sticky business model generating constant cash flow. The proof is in the pudding with a nearly perfect dividend triangle for the past 10 years. I’m adding to my position as ADP was a smaller portion of my investment. It was time to bring it back to my target weight (between 3% and 3.5%).

Buy LeMaitre Vascular (LMAT)

The rest of Starbucks’ proceeds went to another great business that I already hold: LMAT! I love when I can buy a stock with a great dividend triangle with a stock price that goes nowhere. LMAT is down almost 10% year-to-date, while revenue is up 10% and EPS is up 20% so far this year.

Telus (TU) will be sold in our portfolio model

Finally, I can already tell you upfront that our first portfolio model review will happen after one quarter (we started them on October 1st), and Telus will be gone from the Canadian income portfolio. The full review of the four portfolios will be part of a newsletter published in January.

I just wanted to give you a hint of what I plan to have happen with Telus since so many members have it in their portfolio.

Smith Manoeuvre Update

Slowly but surely, the portfolio is taking shape with 12 companies spread across 7 sectors. My goal is to build a portfolio of thriving companies with a solid dividend triangle (e.g., with positive revenue, EPS and dividend growth trends). The current portfolio yield is at 2.23% with a 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 11.75%.

  • The portfolio value is now at $27,739.87
  • The portfolio debt is at $22,000.
  • Monthly contribution is set at $750. ($1,000/month starting in January)
  • The annual income is $618.13, and the projected income is $690.74
  • To report my Smith Manoeuvre, I export the Excel data from my DSR PRO dashboard.

Smith Manoeuvre Portfolio Summary

Here’s my SM portfolio summary as of December 9th, 2025 (before the bell):

$1,000 per month starting in January

I will start 2026 by adding $1,000 to some of my smallest positions (companies like Great-West, Brookfield Infrastructure, etc.).

The point will be to balance the portfolio and then move forward. I also have a few stocks in mind to add next year!

Pension Portfolio Summary

Here’s my pension plan portfolio summary as of December 9th, 2025 (before the bell):

Total value: $323,127.52 (+$301.83 from last month).

Dividend Income: $399.81 (-11.29% VS. November 2024)

I’m down a little this month as I don’t have Texas Instruments and Apple in my portfolio ($78 paid last year). The other companies all increased their dividends since last year.

Since I started this portfolio in September 2017, I have received a total of $33,723.42 CAD in dividends. Keep in mind that this is a “pure dividend growth portfolio,” as no capital can be added to this account other than retained and/or reinvested dividends. Therefore, all dividend growth is coming from the stocks and not from any additional capital being added to the account.

Final Thoughts

I like the moves I made this month for my portfolio. I know my investments are ready to thrive through 2026 with a smile.

I wish you a wonderful Christmas with your friends and family. Don’t worry, I’ll have the investment themes for 2026 ready for next Friday!

data.title: SPY: Structural Risk To Valuation Requires Caution Or Hedges For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Predicting the market is an ungrateful and arduous task that is inevitably incorrect. I prefer to look for risks that could derail historical performance, and 2026 has many threats that may structurally impact US equity valuations, including Fed independence, bond vigilantes, domestic and international politics, and the AI infrastructure buildout. The current SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) has a forward PE of more than 25x; a move back to 16x cannot be disregarded, and risk control via hedges, gold, or emerging-market exposure is warranted.

What Moves Stocks

In general, long-term moves in stocks are based on earnings growth and valuations/interest rates. However, short-term day-to-day volatility is driven by human fear and greed.

Earnings: Have been shown to be resilient, with the bulk of the S&P 500 able to grow EPS in the 8% to 16% range, via a strong economy, exports, innovation, cost control, stock buybacks, etc. While it may be difficult to fathom, disappointing earnings or a reduction in EPS for 500 stocks is rare, and even black swan events such as the pandemic and the Great Financial Crisis have losers and winners. The perception of weak earnings, driven by fears of a recession, typically causes a market decline or stagnation to a greater extent than the actual change in EPS. I don’t fear or worry too much about aggregate SPY earnings growth as long as we are in a democracy, free market, and/or capitalist system.

Valuations: This is more susceptible to disruption, with interest rates the main factor influencing investors. As with many market factors, perception is much more important than facts or numbers. The market will sell down if the Fed hikes rates and vice versa. The rationale is that with lower rates, money moves out of bonds to stocks as investors seek better risk-reward. The more academic view is that lower rates reduce the risk-free rate and net present value increases, i.e., valuations increase. This is generally expressed via a P/E ratio; the higher the P/E ratio, the lower the discount rate. But I am not a fan of DCFs; it's science fiction to project to perpetuity, much less a few years. I believe valuations are perceptions of future value that can change quickly and are susceptible to greed and fear.

Risks to Valuation

Thus, I focus on risk factors that affect investors' perceptions of stock value.

Fed Independence: Mostly a topic in emerging markets, the independence of monetary policy from political influence is paramount to asset valuations. If the Supreme Court rules that a President can fire a Fed chairman, the risks of politicizing monetary policy increase. This would cause rates (risk premium) to increase and drive down stock and everything else.

Fiscal Dominance Backfires: With or without an independent Fed, the US and many nations with high debt-to-GDP ratios and recurring budget deficits need to keep their debt costs low. Austerity, or cutting the deficit, is political suicide, as is raising taxes. To this end, the Treasury and Fed have many tools to drive rates lower, such as pushing banks to buy and hold treasuries, requiring stablecoins to be backed by treasuries, and having the Fed purchase treasuries or mortgage-backed securities, effectively printing money to fund the debt. This works to a point, but only if the investor has faith in the USD and that inflation will not eat into returns. Bond vigilantes, as they are called, can demand higher rates on the long end, which upends stock valuations.

Geopolitical Risk: Unfortunately, many of the Trump administration's foreign and economic policies have weakened the US power base by breaking alliances and contracts, including the soft power of US Aid. This has damaged confidence in the US dollar and increased the risk premium for US debt. Further actions that undermine the rule of law, balance of power (executive, judicial, and legislative), and unilateral actions (leaving NATO, etc.) would further damage the ability to fund deficits at current or lower rates, with asset prices impacted.

AI/Data Center Productivity Risk: While AI, as a productivity tool, and the generative models and data centers required to run it are not likely to be tested in 2026, the productivity or cost of building data centers (GPU/Electricity, etc.) and training models may increase exponentially and leave a vast supply chain from Nvidia to Cameco with severe demand cuts that would reverberate across the market and impact valuations.

The bottom-up analysis

Conducting a bottom-up analysis of 500 stocks is daunting, so I gathered consensus data on the top 70% of AUM (75 stocks). I calculated the weighted upside potential based on analyst price targets and also the EPS growth in the 2026-2027 period. Note that this is not adjusted for stock-based compensation or buybacks. Finally, I calculated the current PE and PEG ratio.

As can be seen in the tables below, the upside for the SPX and the SPY is 10% plus 1% in dividends, which is squarely in line with long-term historical return. Note that the equal-weight SPY has a 12% upside projection, which suggests an ETF such as the State Street® SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® (SPYM) may be an interesting option.

The estimated EPS growth for the SPY is 16% for the 2026-2027 period, down from 25% in 2025, as many of the AI stocks normalize growth rates. At the same time, while this pace is high vs the historical long-term rates of around 8%, it's in line with the tech-dominated era of 16%.

Finally, the weighted-average PE for 2026 is 25.7x, while the consensus price targets need to be expanded to 28.1x. The positive news is that the relative valuation, or PEG, is 1.4x, which, to me, is reasonable or not alarming in the current low-rate environment. Keep in mind that the SPY is a moving portfolio, with higher-valued stocks and presumably higher-growth companies gaining weight in the index.

Conclusion

Corrections are fine; they mean the drop is temporary, and I assume 2026 will have one or more months with 5% to 10% down. If none of the risk events occur, then the SPY may reach $759. However, structural changes in valuation, as discussed in this article, could produce a reset for stock prices (and other assets). Where today's 26x PE is fine and accepted, a move to 16x would trigger a nasty crash, and recovery would require a fix to whatever was structurally changed. In the current environment, this cannot be discarded or assigned a low probability. Hedging the stock portfolio with Gold or an inverse ETF and diversifying into emerging markets that benefit from a weak dollar are all sound ideas of risk control.

data.title: Edenor: Recovery Is Real, But The Market Is Already Pricing It In
data.description:
When I wrote my first note of this year about Edenot (EDN), the idea was clear: the company was starting to show recovery signals after several complicated years, but the regulatory and political context was a central risk factor.

Later on, with the H1 results, I analyzed the stock again, and even though I have seen improvements, these were backed by extraordinary factors and a fragile macro environment.

Since then, EDN has had a strong upside move. This increase is not explained by a punctual quarter, rather by a wider change in the expectations about Argentina after Milei’s government won the midterm elections, which allowed the rise of the number of deputies and senators. The aim is to obtain a quorum for voting on government law projects and to move forward with pro-market reforms.

Edenor, as one of the most punished utilities over the years, has ended up as one of the most benefited from that change in market sentiment. The objective of this article is not to explain that rally, rather to see if the results of the third quarter begin to endorse that new lecture.

What does Q3 show?

The third quarter does not show a turning point, but it allows us to reach continuity. In Q3 2025, Edenor had revenues of AR$741B, with a real improvement near 1% YoY. There are no more sudden jumps in tariffs, but monthly and automatic adjustments that give more predictability to the business after twenty years of frozen tariffs: these were not adjusted regularly in a high inflation context, making distortions within earnings of the electricity-provider companies.

The distribution margin reached AR$311B, 12% higher than Q3 2024, even with higher energy costs due to subsidy reduction. In this context, EBITDA became, again, the most relevant fact: during the quarter, it reached AR$133B, more than double one year ago. Meanwhile, in the first nine months of the year, the accumulated EBITDA was of AR$440B, with a 141% YoY growth.

The most important thing is that this improvement does not depend just on the agreement with CAMMESA, because, taking out the extraordinary effect, accumulated EBITDA was AR$239B, an increase of 31% YoY. This shows us that recovery begins to keep on the operation and not only in countable adjustments.

In operative terms, the client base reached up to 3.38M, with a 2% year-over-year growth and energy sales of 5,958 GWh, little lower with respect to the past year. At the same time, AR$283 B executed in the first nine months, in line with the plan approved by the regulator.

These investments still have an impact on the service quality. In Q3, indicators such as SAIDI and SAIFI were 7.1 hours and 2.8 cuts on average per client, which shows better levels than the historicals and are kept below the regulatory limits.

High growth, profitability, yet low

If we look at growth, EDN still stands out from the sector because consensus waits for an earnings growth near 40%, actually over the utilities average. We also observe that expected growth for EBITDA is kept in double digits, near 20%.

The problem pops up when we analyze profitability, because, even with the improvement of this year, margins are still low. The EBITDA margin is about 11%, in front of higher levels within the sector, and the EBIT margin is still being reduced, which explains why growth is not translated into stable profits.

This results mix is what distorts the forward multiples. Expected P/E is at high levels, over 600x. Not a typical sign that shares are pricey, rather a sample of what little trustable are profit estimations there are on this step, affected by risk, inflation, countable normalization, and the disappearance of extraordinary earnings.

Looking at operative metrics, Edenor still shows discounts. EV/EBITDA rounds to 9x, compared to a sectorial median near 13x, and P/B is still low regarding comparables, which shows us that the market applies an important discount due to sovereign and regulatory risks.

2026 Scenarios: what discounts the market and why

With a share trading at around $31.5, and taking as a base the Q3 2025 results with the consensus expectations, I have elaborated three scenarios for 2026, to estimate in which price range the share could be for next year.

In each case I went from the same base. Consensus expects actually high growth regarding the sector, with earnings growing near 40%, and EBITDA still heading to double digits. At the same time, margins are still low, and multiples of forward profits are completely distorted. With that turning point, I ask myself whether it is sustainable growth and how much risk the market is disposed to pay.

In the most conservative scenario, I assume growth is slowing down and margins are not better; therefore, in this case, I do not see reasons for the market’s changing its actual perception. Even though Edenor left behind the worst, it is still listed at a high discount, so the upside could be actually limited, in the range of $30-$33.

The base scenario is the one more reasonable today. In this case, I replicate what happened in Q3, without extraordinary effects, with tariff adjustments functioning and growing over the sector but moderated. I do not see a profitability jump, but yes, more predictability; therefore, in that context, it seems logical to think in a valuation that is more organized, with a moderate upside since the actual price but without exaggerating, so I expect it could be situated around $36-$38.

Meanwhile, my expectations for the bull scenario are more dependent on external factors. I suppose that the regulatory setting will consolidate, macro will pair with it, and the market is beginning to demand less risk premium. On this projection I do not consider a margin-comparables-with-the-sector scenario, but one with less risk perception, which could lead EDN to a higher revaluation until $42-$45.

Risks that do not disappear

Even Q3 numbers show a clear improvement, Edenor still being an asset where risk weights. The main risk is the regulatory. And even if what we see today is that the tariff scheme works and gives some predictability, in Argentina that is not guaranteed. A change in the tariff politics could have a direct impact on earnings and take.

There is an operative risk that does not disappear. Even though EDN invests and improves the service, energy losses are still high, and cutting them down takes time. If that process is slower than expected, margins could remain under pressure.

For last, after the rally mentioned in the beginning of this article, expectations are high. That makes any deviation, even a little one, reflected fast on price. Therefore, I consider that even though history has gotten better, the error margin is fenced.

Conclusion

Even the third quarter does not explain the rally Edenor has had; it shows that expectations changing in favor of Argentina were not completely disconnected to operative reality. So, besides an increase explained by Milei’s triumph in the October elections, I must point out that the facts show us that EDN has left behind its most critical stage and heads to a gradual normalization, still incomplete and sensible to the context.

With a moderate upside in the base scenario I have estimated for 2026 and a risk profile that is still high, I keep my Hold rating. Edenor is not a survival story, but it does not offer an attractive risk-return relationship to justify a buy: must keep an eye on the ball. For investors with a medium- to long-term horizon, it still is a position to hold, and to keep an eye on the next quarters.

data.title: PEY Is Not As Bad As It Looks
data.description:
I continue to evaluate exchange-traded funds (ETFs) for investors, and I introduced coverage on a couple of dividend-focused ETFs with Sell ratings, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG). I also introduced a Buy rating for ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV). These are all very very different despite having the word "dividend" in their fund names. Today, I look at another dividend-focused ETF, Invesco High Yield Equity Dividend Achievers ETF (PEY) and find it to be superior to the two that I have rated Sell.

PEY Has Performed Poorly

PEY is focused on high dividend yields, but it is focused on large-caps. The ETF has dropped in price by 3.3% in 2025, while the S&P 500 has jumped 17.5% higher. Including dividends, the return has been slightly positive for PEY:

While the ETF is above the 52-week low it set in April, it is below its 52-week high set in March and the all-time high set in late 2024:

It is currently 87.3% above the low set in early 2020 at $10.97, which is the current 10-year low price. Since inception more than 21 years ago, it has returned 233.8% with dividends, though this is way below the 761.4% return in the S&P 500 since then.

PEY Is Different

Invesco has a page on its website dedicated to PEY. The fund, which was launched in 2004, has 50 stocks in it currently. It is based upon the NASDAQ US Dividend Achievers™ 50 Index. It appears to be the only ETF tracking this index, which is the top 50 securities that are in the NASDAQ US Broad Dividend Achievers™ Index, and it currently has a bit over $1 billion in assets under management. The ETF and its index are reconstituted annually in March, and they are rebalanced quarterly. It pays a monthly dividend and just went ex-dividend.

While it is a passive ETF, the expense ratio is 0.54%, which is nowhere close to the rock-bottom fees that approach zero that many ETFs charge. To be fair, the assets under management are much lower than these other passive ETFs.

Here are the Top 10 holdings as of 12/23:

The first thing that hits me when seeing this is that these are all large names, some of which I do follow. The Top 10 represent 30.3% of the ETF, and there are Here is the exposure by GICS sector as of 11/30:

While I just upgraded State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) from Sell to Hold, I am not a big fan of this part of the market. I remain negative on State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF- (XLF). What I like a lot about the exposure is that it is just 2.9% Technology.

As I stated above, this is the fourth dividend-focused ETF that I have evaluated here. I have not written about iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY) yet, but I am including it as well. I am also including the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Here is PEY compared to the others by several metrics:

PEY is not as widely followed by Seeking Alpha readers as some of these other ETFs, though more people follow it than SMDV, which is focused on smaller stocks. The dividend yield is substantially higher than the S&P 500, and it is above all of the peers here. The year-to-date return is much lower than some of the peers and SPY. In fact, it is the lowest return here. The 14.1% total return over the past three years is the lowest as well. The number of stocks, at 50 (like its index), is the lowest too.

PEY is rated Hold by Seeking Alpha analysts (based on just one article in the last three months, though this rating has been pretty regularly given over the past year). There has been 1 Sell rating in 2025 and a Strong Sell in late 2023. The last time any Seeking Alpha analyst gave this ETF a Buy was in October 2023.

Seeking Alpha currently gives this a Quant rating of 1.82, which is a sell. Here are the ETF grades:

The Momentum score is hurting it, though it has improved over the past three months. The Risk is another factor weighing it down. The Expenses, which are a lot higher than many other ETFs, are weighing it down a bit too. To me, it is kind of surprising to see Dividends scored at a B. Clicking on the that factor, it's pretty clear that the consecutive years of dividend growth is hurting the score as is the dividend growth rate.

Comparing it to the other dividend ETFs, they have Quant ratings that are the lowest, with VIG at 3.50, DVY at 2.83, SCHD at 2.50 and SMDV at 2.13.

PEY Has a Big Management Fee

As I showed above, PEY's annual expenses of 0.54% is the highest of the five dividend ETFs I have discussed. The actual management fee is 0.4%. This may turn off some investors, though it does not seem that high to me. DVY is extremely popular and has only a slightly lower fee, while SMDV, which I like, has an even higher fee than DVY.

I would like to see Invesco lower the fee, but I don't think that investors should expect them to do so unless the ETF becomes larger. I recently initiated Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP) with a Hold, and it has an expense ratio of 0.2%. That ETF, though, has $76.4 billion assets under management. The firm has 161 different ETFs, many of which are focused on the US, and some have lower expenses despite being smaller ETFs. A larger fund that Invesco runs, the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Technology ETF (RSPT) has a 0.4% management fee and 0.4% net expense ratio, but it has $4.1 billion in assets. On dividend-focused ETFs, Invesco runs Invesco Dow Jones Industrial Average Dividend ETF (DJD), which has a net expense ratio of 0.07% but assets under management of just $385 million. It runs an even smaller fund, Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Growers ETF (DIVG), at 0.39% management fee and 0.39% net expense ratio.

I believe that investors should pay close attention to the expenses of their mutual fund and ETF investments, but it should not be the sole factor. The 0.4% management fee and 0.53% net expenses is high for a passive fund, though I don't think that investors should rule out this ETF due to its expenses.

Why You Should Consider PEY

The ETF has underperformed the market as measured by the S&P 500 and could do better relative to the market going forward. The S&P 500 is very exposed to Technology and to the Magnificent 7, and these represent a very high percentage of the index and are responsible for its very good performance since the end of 2022. The top 10 holdings in the S&P 500 currently represent 39.2% of the index, with Technology currently at 34.5%.I like several things about PEY, including that it is not that widely followed at Seeking Alpha, that its size is big enough but not that big, and that its Quantitative score is so low compared to peers. I don't think that either of these three factors alone will boost it, but they do suggest that anyone who wants to take a contrarian position could experience a big favorable move if they are right.

Many Seeking Alpha commenters seem to be quite negative on dividends. I don't see anything inherently wrong with dividends, though I am not so excited by dividends either. I believe that all companies must make important decisions about their capital, including how they spend it on dividends and on share repurchases, how they reinvest it in the business and how they spend it on M&A. There is more to capital spending than just dividends!

The index for PEY has just 50 members. I don't view this as too small, but there is an annual reconstitution risk coming up in just three months. As I said above, the index is part of a larger index, and Invesco runs an ETF dedicated to matching that index. The Invesco Dividend Achievers ETF (PFM), which has 429 stocks and a net expense ratio of 0.52%, has returned 15.2% in 2025. It has outpaced PEY over the past decade too, returning 206.4%.

So, the weak returns for PEY may be due to the index, and investors will need to determine if this is a permanent issue or one that has been temporary. Looking at the Top 10 names, which I shared above, the year-to-date performance has been horrible for all. Only two have increased in 2025, and the best one is up just 12.8%, which is less than the S&P 500. The eight that are down have declined by 2.9% to as much as 60.9%:

The average decline has been 26.3% in 2025, with an 11.3% return quarter-to-date.The list is sorted by ticker alphabetically, but the top three have yields of 7.0% or higher and a total of five yield 7% or more.

I do cover a couple of these ten largest names currently in PEY, but I don't think that investors should be concerned about the ETF because of its current holdings. As I said above, it will be reconstituted in March. Some of the weakness in these largest holdings may be due to year-end tax-loss selling, which will end in just a few days. It is pretty clear to me that some of these dividend yields are too high and that some of these companies have very bad balance sheets.

Conclusion

I found PEY in an article about good dividend ETFs and bad ones, as the article pointed out how bad PEY is in the author's view. After having written about three dividend ETFs, I decided to dig deeper. While the performance has been unappealing recently, and the fee is somewhat high relative to peers, it is run by a good manager, Invesco, and it has done a good job of tracking its index. I don't see it as a Buy right now, but this is mainly because I am concerned about stocks and bonds right now, as I have expressed in several articles. I rate this ETF as a Hold. I do believe that investors who are more bullish than I am should consider this ETF, and that investors in SCHD and VIG, upon which I have Sell ratings, and DVY should contemplate reducing or exiting in favor of buying PEY. I prefer SMDV

data.title: Bridgewater Bancshares: I Disagree With The Market's Decision To Push Shares Higher
data.description:
In July of this year, I did something I rarely do. This was not just a downgrade. It was a double downgrade. That's right. I downgraded shares of Bridgewater Bancshares (BWB) from a ‘strong buy’ all the way down to a ‘hold’. This came after the stock had skyrocketed, rising 53.6% compared to the 40.5% rise that the S&P 500 had seen. After that jump, I believed that the company justified a more cautious approach. To be honest with you, the market has disagreed with me since then. It seems to believe that I downgraded too soon. I say this because, since that event, shares are up 15.3%. This is almost double the 8.1% rise that the market has enjoyed.

Looking at the data provided, I can understand why the market has pushed back some. Revenue and profits are both on the rise. The balance sheet of the company is growing slightly. And relative to other similar firms, the financial institution does not look ridiculously priced. But between asset quality and valuation, I stand by my decision to downgrade it to the ‘hold’ that I ultimately downgraded it to. And I firmly believe that, moving forward, it is unlikely to perform materially different than the broader market.

Still not banking on Bridgewater Bancshares

When it comes to Banks, I always like to start with the balance sheet. The most recent data that we have covers the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, deposits amounted to $4.29 billion. That's actually up nicely from the $4.09 billion reported for the end of 2020 or. And it represents the smallest increase over the $4.24 billion the company had in the second quarter of this year. The good news is that the recent increase was driven almost entirely by organic deposits. Brokered deposits, which are high cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions, grew only slightly from $825.8 million at the end of last year to $834.4 million this year. The real driver for the institution, then, ended up being savings and money market deposits that jumped from $1.26 billion to $1.43 billion. Regardless, the fact that this is organic growth is great to see in and of itself.

Another thing that I view in a slightly positive light is the uninsured deposit exposure of the institution. You see, I typically like to see no more than 30% of all deposits classified as uninsured. The company ended the most recent quarter with 29.2% of all deposits hitting this category. Although this is below the 30% maximum threshold that I typically like to see, it is worth noting that the year over year increase was substantial. A year ago, only 25% of deposits were uninsured. So investors should watch this carefully. After all, these are deposits that would be susceptible to a real bank run should times get tough. And ideally, we should want as little exposure here as possible.

As deposits increased, the value of loans that the company has grew also. The firm ended 2024 with $3.81 billion worth of loans. But today, they stand at $4.15 billion. The greatest exposure that the institution has here would be the $1.58 billion of multifamily real estate mortgages. But other major areas of exposure would be the $1.16 billion of commercial real estate non-owner-occupied assets, the $533.5 million of commercial loans, and the $487.3 million of one to four family residential real estate.

One thing that I am always concerned about when I see a large amount of exposure to commercial real estate that is non-owner-occupied would be exposure to office assets specifically. This is because office vacancy rates are rising. And vacant properties can't pay rent. The good news is that only $211 million of the overall loan portfolio of the company, or 5% of all of its loans outstanding, fall under this category. And with a weighted average loan to value ratio of 61%, these loans are likely low risk.

Although the vast majority of its assets are tied up in loans like most banks, Bridgewater Bancshares does have some exposure when it comes to securities. These totaled $826.5 million for the third quarter. That's actually up from the $768.2 million the company ended last year with. Over the same window of time, cash and cash equivalents actually contracted from $234.1 million to $135.5 million. And debt went from $452.9 million to $513.1 million. When you put all of this together, the pattern is clear. Management is shifting capital into loans and securities, even if it means having lower cash balances and slightly higher debt. The goal here is to optimize returns. And honestly, the firm seems to be doing a pretty good job on that front.

For the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, net interest income was $33 million. That's up nicely from the $25.6 million reported a year earlier. Some of this rise absolutely came from the change in the size of the company's balance sheet. But it wasn't just the size that made a difference. The company also saw a change in the composition of its balance sheet that helped. As a result, net interest margin grew from 2.24% last year to 2.63% this year. It certainly helped that a decline in interest rates allowed the company to reduce how much it was paying depositors.

Interest bearing transaction deposits saw a decline from 4.63% to 3.78%. Time deposits saw dropped from 4.39% to 4.28%. And savings and money market deposits dropped meaningfully from 4.34% to 3.63%. This makes sense. After all, immediate changes in the interest rate environment necessitate, or I guess I should say justify, changes to anything that would be involved in the spot market from an interest rate standpoint. By comparison, investments being made by the institution can be adjusted lower at a slower pace, especially if those are fixed rate instruments.

This rise in net interest income was not the only area of improvement that the company saw. Non-interest income actually grew from $1.5 million to $2.1 million. And this, according to management, was driven primarily by an extra $0.2 million of investment advisory fee revenue and an increase in customer service fee revenue from $0.4 million to $0.5 million. Thanks to these changes, net profits jumped year over year from $7.7 million to $10.6 million. And for the first nine months of this year in its entirety, the $29.7 million in net income generated by the company exceeded the $21.6 million reported for last year.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we can see in the chart above that Bridgewater Bancshares is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 12.2. This is unfortunately above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. But relative to other banks, it's not expensive. In that same chart, you can see how it stacks up against five such firms. Two of them are cheaper than it is on this front. We get a similar outcome when looking at the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see what I mean. Although four of the five banks are cheaper than our candidate is on a price to book basis, only two of them are cheaper when it comes to the price to tangible book approach.

Another thing that's worth mentioning would be asset quality. Return on assets is one good example. I typically like a reading here of 1% or greater. But as the first chart below illustrates, Bridgewater Bancshares has a reading right now of 0.88%. That also puts the company below three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. In this case, the company is closer to being attractive. I typically prefer a reading here of 10% or greater. And at 9.77%, it is certainly knocking on that door. But once again, three of the five banks are superior to it on this front.

Credit quality should also not be ignored when evaluating this opportunity. From the end of last year to the third quarter of this year, there was a pretty substantial increase in non-performing loans as a percent of total loans. That increase was from 0.01% to 0.24%. However, that is still far lower than the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. So in this regard, I would say that Bridgewater Bancshares is doing a fine job.

Takeaway

Taken all together, Bridgewater Bancshares most certainly is not a bad candidate. But I just don't think it's a great one. The income statement and balance sheet looked positive. The company is clearly doing a fine job of restructuring certain aspects of its balance sheet in order to create more value for shareholders. Declining interest rates are also helping it in the near term. But when it comes to asset quality and valuation, it's just a decent candidate. And as somebody looking to outperform the market, I believe that there are better opportunities out there. That's why I think that maintaining this as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right move.

data.title: Portland General Electric: A Regulated Utility With A Narrow Moat
data.description:
Article Thesis

2024 and 2025 were two great years for utilities, with short-term tailwinds like lower interest rates and mid-to-long term tailwinds like increased demand for electricity driven especially by data centers.

For 2024, even a passive investment in State Street® Utilities Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLU) would have brought 21.5%, a rare feast for this defensive sector. This is mostly because the market was already positioning itself ahead of the Fed's interest rate easing cycle. For some months, XLU brought even higher returns than S&P 500, and that in the middle of the AI frenzy:

With more focus on certain utilities that benefited disproportionately from the data center demand, like the Southern US ones, we could have easily surpassed the S&P 500.

The positive trend for utilities continued in 2025, but with a lower total return of 14.5%. Besides, I already found most of the Southern US ones slightly overvalued; that’s why I looked mostly for diversified options strategies, because these strategies can still bring high returns even with slight movements in their underlying stocks:

  • A bull Call Spread (long Call option, short lower strike Call) in Evergy (EVRG), which brought ~30.5% return in about three months.
  • A short naked Put in Avista Corporation (AVA), with ~24% return in about five and a half months.
  • A long Call option in Portland General Electric (POR), with ~52% return in about six months.

I will detail in the following the mechanics of this last position and why I see Portland General Electric only as a “Hold” today.

Business Highlights

Portland General Electric, or PGE, is an integrated electric utility company, with operations in the state of Oregon. I like that there is a balanced customer load mix: commercial, industrial, and residential. And the industrial load is also mixed, not just data centers and AI:

Total retail energy deliveries for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 increased 5% YoY, driven by the Industrial segment (of course, with its data centers) with 18% growth. However, the Industrial segment had a much lower contribution in total revenues, only 15%, which means that only about half this growth will translate into revenues.

Like any other regulated utility, PGE enjoys a narrow economic moat due to its natural monopoly. But again, like any other regulated utility, we can’t speak about a strong economic moat, because their returns are capped by regulators. With only one jurisdiction, maybe PGE is slightly riskier than its peers.

Their approved ROE (Return On Equity) of 9.34% is close to the US average, but their realized ROE is lower, at ~8%, with a five-years average of 8.03%. This underperformance is mostly a result of their wholesale operations (buying or selling electricity through long-term and short-term contracts), with energy deliveries down 6% YoY.

Financial Highlights

In Q3’25, PGE reported a $0.94 GAAP EPS, compared to $0.90 one year ago, and a $1 non-GAAP EPS. But much more important is their mid-term outlook. And for a regulated utility, this is quite reliable, because it’s based on their negotiated CapEx and rate base growth:

Let’s not forget, however, their wholesale business segment (10%-15% of total revenues), with a not-so-great performance recently. Therefore, I would model EPS growth closer to the lower end of this range, to be a bit on the conservative side.

Dividend growth should follow closely, because they have a healthy payout ratio of ~65%, with 60%-70% considered healthy for a regulated utility.

Finally, PGE has an investment-grade credit rating from both S&P and Moody’s, BBB+ being in line with other regulated utilities:

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, I look at a comparison with PGE’s five-year averages:

But, as I always said, not all multiples are born equal. For regulated utilities I look at:

  • Price / Book: completely irrelevant for a growth stock, but arguably the most relevant for a regulated utility, because it’s close to Price / Rate Base. We have 8%-9% undervaluation, from 1.33x today compared to 1.45 5Y average.
  • EV/EBITDA FWD: again, very important for regulated utilities because it includes profitability and debt, an essential part of their business model. We have about the same value as the 5Y average.

Looking at the same multiples for the sector, we get about 43% and 28% apparent undervaluation, but this is misleading. We need to combine this with their lower ROE, 17%-18% lower than the sector. Therefore, we get maybe half of that undervaluation or even less.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages:

Div growth

Fair Value

$50.77

With a Fair Value estimate of $50.77, PGE looks about 7% undervalued compared to the current price ($47.46), and this is backed by the relative valuation.

I usually rate a regulated utility as a “Buy” if the undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth is greater than 20%. And while we had about 33% back in June, it’s only 7% + 4.4% + 5.2% = 16.6% today, therefore a “Hold”.

Strategy

With a 33% undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth in June, PGE was not quite a “Strong Buy” at $40.15, but that was enough for me to initiate a leveraged strategy through an ITM (In-The-Money) Call option: PGE 25 Call expiring Dec-19, 2025, for $15.16 per share, or $1516 per contract.

On November 11, as posted through a comment, with the stock just slightly above $50, I also sold a POR 50 Call Dec’25 for $99. Why did I do that? Because the stock was already around my Fair Value estimate, and I wanted to protect my already big gains from the 25 Call against a pullback. And indeed, the stock retreated a little, to $47.46 today.

The long 25 Call has been exercised just at its expiration, for the dividend from Dec-22, and the short 50 Call expired worthless, which gives us the following results, considering today’s stock price of $47.46:

*This short Call should normally decrease the cost base, since I cashed in a premium, but it was sold very late, so I'll keep modeling the initial cost.

**Please notice that, in a cash-type account, one share would cost $25, therefore above the initial $15.16. That’s why I wouldn’t buy these shares for the dividend, but I would just sell the Call option 2-3 days before its expiration. And this is also what I usually do in a margin-type account, because this would give a very close result.

However, in a margin-type account, I don’t have this constraint of all those $25 per share being blocked, but much less, and therefore the maximum cost is the initial $15.16 per share paid for the Call option. I do look, however, at my total cash balance after buying those shares to be positive, because otherwise I would pay interest. That’s why, with plenty of cash, I sometimes buy those shares for the dividend, for a couple of extra-dollars, although this is not a given, because the stock price usually retreats on the ex-dividend date (and this is why the result is very close to the scenario when I sold my long Call option just before its expiration), so:

  • I closed half-position at $47.46.
  • I am hoping for a further comeback in the next 2-3 days.

Risks

Since I am already closing my position, I don’t want to speak too much about risks, but I do want to explain the reward/risk for that bull Call position. This deep ITM Call option has about the same leverage in both directions, ~2.6X. Therefore, the risk on the downside is substantial.

But let’s see a bit more: what is the main risk for PGE with the stock at $40.15, a 5.2% dividend yield and a 5%+ dividend growth. Yes, sure, there is this drag from their wholesale business, but I already incorporated that by modelling only 5.1% dividend growth, very close to their lower edge.

The main risk, and more difficult to quantify, is wildfire risk. However, back in June, there were only about 2-3 months left with this risk, and PGE also has a solid wildfire risk management:

In conclusion, I saw this risk with a very low probability in comparison with the possible reward. PGE had ~23% undervaluation and an abnormally higher dividend for a regulated utility, considering its estimated growth and an interest rate easing cycle, which made the reward/risk for this leveraged position appear very favorable.

Takeaway

Portland General Electric is an integrated electric utility company, with operations in the state of Oregon. PGE's regulated utility model offers a narrow economic moat, but underperforming wholesale operations (10%-15% of revenues) and over-reliance on industrial customers (1/3 of energy deliveries) increase the uncertainty.

A DDM derived Fair Value, and backed by relative valuation, is $50.77, suggesting PGE is about 7% undervalued at $47.46, with a healthy 4.4% yield and a healthy ~65% payout ratio. PGE is rated 'Hold' as current undervaluation plus dividend yield and growth total only 16.6%, below my 20% buy threshold.

A long POR 25 Call Dec '25 initiated in June, complemented with a short POR 50 Call Dec'25 in November, delivered ~58% ROI in ~6 months, but with valuation normalization and moderate growth, new positions are less compelling.

data.title: Rent the Runway: Back In Growth Mode, But Profitability Remains The Key Question
data.description:
Rent the Runway (RENT) returned to growth in its rental revenue through new strategies and inventory expansion, resulting in a customer base that surpasses its 2019 level. However, costs are increasing faster than revenues, and margins compressed in the most recent quarter, which puts the sustainability of this growth into question unless costs are brought under control.

The Company

RENT gives access to designer clothes at a fraction of the price. It offers three types of services: subscription, reservation (or one-off rental), and resale. In the subscription model, customers subscribe and receive access to five to ten different items of clothing for a wide range of situations, from workwear to loungewear, casual, maternity, to accessories, jewelry, handbags, and other items. Customers also have the option to keep the items for longer if they like them, or return them and select other clothes. The reserve model is a one-off rental with a period of 4 to 8 days. On the resale model, customers have the option to buy the used clothes at a discount, which is a category that is growing substantially in revenue, and does not require a subscription.

The company acquires products through wholesale, share by RTR (Rent the Runway), and exclusive designs. The wholesale segment represents about 30% of product acquisition, where the company buys from brands in volume and at discounted prices. In the Share by RTR segment, the company pays nothing upfront, and the brands receive a portion of the net profits over time. However, over time, the brands can end up receiving more than if RENT were just to buy the piece, and this segment represents about 48% of product acquisition. The company also partners with brands to manufacture exclusive designs based on its customers preferences and to have more durable pieces. Exclusive designs account for about 22% of the company’s product acquisition.

The business model divides consumers' opinions. On one hand, some love the variety and the thrill of wearing something new every so often. On the other hand, others see it as a waste of money, arguing that they would rather spend that same amount on pieces they can keep. Still, the model is definitely a conversation starter, which leads to a more organic growth for the company.

Recent developments

In the Q3 2025 results, RENT converted more than 50% of its debt into equity, invested in inventory, created an affiliated program to drive organic growth, improved its homepage algorithm, leading to a 70% increase in click-through rates, and increased transparency with a clearer add-on pricing experience. These initiatives have not only improved liquidity but have also demonstrated higher conversion rates. In my view, these developments are very positive and position the company for continued organic growth in the coming quarters.

Financials

Looking at the company’s history, the business suffered in the 2020 pandemic period, losing about 58% of its customer base, from more than 133 thousand in 2019 to about 55 thousand in 2020. However, the pandemic period is in the past now, and in the last quarterly release, the company reported almost 149 thousand customers, above pre-pandemic levels, which is also a 12% YoY increase in the customer base from the quarter. Subscription and Reserve rental revenue increased 16% YoY, while revenue from the sale of items, which is the other revenue on the income statement, increased 10% YoY. This revenue from the sale of items is on a strong upward trend, more than doubling since 2022. A side note here: I think this is funny, because in some of the videos I watched of consumers sharing their experience with the company, they felt the most excited not by having as many options to rent, but by seeing a cheap sale price for an item they liked; they were very inclined to buy it, and this relates to what is happening in the company’s revenues.

On the cost side, total costs and expenses increased 15.8% YoY, from $89.6 million in Q3 2024 to $103.8 million in Q3 2025. Most of this cost increase was from rental product depreciation and revenue share (payments for the brands), which increased 33.6% YoY, from $28.2 million to $37.7 million. When comparing this $37.7 million expense with the $32.9 million depreciation charge on the income statement, which decreased from a figure of $35.5 million a year earlier, it becomes clear that the company is paying more to the brands than before, while depreciation costs have remained nearly the same. This highlights a less favorable cost structure for the company, which is a negative point for investors to keep an eye on. On the positive side, SG&A increased 7% YoY, from $21.2 million to $22.7 million, which is less than the total revenue increase in percentage terms. This resulted in an operating loss of $16.2 million, lower than the previous year's $13.7 million operational loss. The company also had a one-time gain of $96.3 million, which was from the debt restructuring. This turned a would-be net loss of $19.8 million into a net income of $76.5 million. On the balance sheet, the company is losing money, but the debt situation has improved. Cash decreased YoY from $77.4 million to $50.7 million, while total debt decreased from $373.7 million in Q3 2024 to $203.1 million in Q3 2025.

Competitors

RENT’s main competitor is Nuuly. RENT stands out for its luxury designer event outfits, while Nuuly focuses on everyday style variety with greater size inclusivity. Nuuly is the market leader despite starting later, in 2019, and it has surpassed RENT's customer base in just a few years. Nuuly is owned by Urban Outfitters (URBN), which allows it to source inventory at cost from URBN's sister brands, but it also has an enormous variety of brands available for consumers to choose from. The strategy of catering to a more everyday style is working better for Nuuly since it charges less than RENT, and Nuuly’s subscriber count is growing much faster. The recent changes in RENT's strategy are promising, but the inventory increase cited by RENT's management seems to also be targeting a more everyday style (according to internet comments), which is not really RENT's competitive advantage. As things stand today, it seems that Nuuly may continue to have the advantage over RENT due to a lower cost to the consumer and a broader target audience, while RENT still needs to prove it can be profitable.

Valuation

I think the best metric to use with RENT is the EV/Sales, as it accounts for the company's capital structure, including debt. The company is trading at 1.42x EV/Sales while the sector median is 1.38x. So, not much apparent upside there. The company is also trading at 3.74x P/E GAAP (TTM) while the sector median is 20.25x. However, that is due to the on-off revenue from debt restructuring, making the company reach a positive net income in the quarter, but this revenue will not show up in the next quarter, and the company may continue to have negative earnings. RENT is also trading at a Price/Sales of 0.12x, versus the sector median of 0.95x, which is very cheap, but in my view, it will only matter if the company successfully reduces costs, or manages to increase revenues faster than costs.

Risks

The fashion industry is competitive, fragmented, and rapidly changing. Shifting consumer preferences and trends or a shift away from subscription models in favor of ownership or other alternatives could reduce the customer base. The company’s specific model could also be replicated by the very brands it partners with. Consumer retention is also a challenge; the company must keep the clothes feeling like new as they pass from consumer to consumer. For example, if the company keeps a piece in circulation for too long, it could make it feel worn or dated, disappointing customers and harming the company’s reputation. Conversely, if the company renews its stock too often, the costs would be unsustainable, so, it’s a hard balance, and executing this wrong could hurt profitability.

Conclusion

While the company is moving forward with renewed strategies, reaching more clients, and seeing the results in revenue increases, the cost side is increasing faster. I think the best move is to wait and see if the company can grow revenues faster than costs, which is why I view the stock as a “Hold”.

data.title: John Hancock Fundamental Large Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks rallied strongly during the third quarter of 2025, ending on a high note that offset some short-lived sell-offs and spikes of volatility throughout the period.
  • Within the broad-based S&P 500 Index (SP500), the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors notched the biggest gains, while the consumer staples and real estate sectors were the biggest laggards.
  • Stock picks in the IT, energy, and healthcare sectors hampered performance versus the S&P 500 Index during the quarter.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks rallied strongly during the third quarter of 2025, ending on a high note that offset some short-lived sell-offs and spikes of volatility throughout the period. Softer-than-expected economic data, especially in the labor market, and the first interest rate cut of the year in September—thanks to an increasingly more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve—helped fuel investors' appetite for equities. Corporate earnings strength also provided markets with confidence that the economic backdrop could support further stock gains. A wave of AI deals announced in September was another driving force. Within the broad-based benchmark, the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors notched the biggest gains, while the consumer staples and real estate sectors were the biggest laggards.

Contributors and detractors

Stock picks in the IT, energy, and healthcare sectors hampered performance versus the benchmark in Q3. The biggest individual detractor was a lack of exposure to NVIDIA Corp., the dominant player in the AI chip market. A non-index stake in liquefied natural gas company Cheniere Energy (LNG) also detracted as investors sold energy stocks on the back of abnormally low oil prices. Elsewhere, a non-index position in brewer and spirits supplier Anheuser-Busch (BUD) also detracted. The company's share price sank after reporting financial results that fell short of investors' expectations due to weakening demand for its products in the United States and falling sales volumes in key international markets.

Conversely, security selection in the industrials sector helped the strategy's relative performance. Additional boosts came from an overweight position in the communication services sector, as well as an underweight position in financials. Top individual contributors to relative performance included an overweight position in Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG), which benefited from earnings that exceeded expectations. The company was also boosted in September by news that it avoided harsh antitrust penalties in the United States. Another standout was U.S.-based equipment rental company United Rentals (URI), whose shares rallied on the back of solid revenue results and expectations that the company would benefit from the government's One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which gives companies more financial incentives to build infrastructure. Also of note was a non-index position in Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which is expected to benefit from continued strong demand for chips with AI applications.

Portfolio changes

Portfolio changes in Q3 included eliminated holdings in healthcare, real estate, IT, and communication services. We directed proceeds to new holdings in the consumer discretionary, communication services, IT, and healthcare sectors. The team sees opportunity across the style spectrum and seeks to remain relatively balanced. We're currently seeing more opportunities and marginal tilts toward value.
